did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a fuzzy-based SegNet model (combining deep fuzzy clustering and SegNet) for skin lesion segmentation and a normalized stacked Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network for classifying melanoma skin cancer lesions. It also employs ResNet-50 for hybrid feature extraction. These AI/deep learning methods were applied to dermoscopic images from the ISIC-2017 and PH2 datasets to detect and classify melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel automated system combining a fuzzy-based SegNet model and a normalized stacked LSTM network for melanoma detection. It details original methodology, dataset usage (ISIC-2017 and PH2), experimental implementation, and reports specific empirical results (e.g., 98.98% accuracy on ISIC-2017), which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Accurate detection of melanoma skin cancer using fuzzy based SegNet model and normalized stacked LSTM network
journal: Indonesian Journal of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated system for the early detection of melanoma skin cancer using dermoscopic images from the ISIC-2017 and PH2 datasets. The methodology employs a fuzzy-based SegNet model, which combines deep fuzzy clustering with SegNet, for precise skin lesion segmentation. Hybrid feature extraction is then performed using the ResNet-50 model and the local tri-directional pattern (LTriDP) descriptor. These extracted features are classified into specific lesion classes using a normalized stacked Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. The empirical evaluation demonstrates that the proposed normalized stacked LSTM network achieves high accuracy rates of 98.98% on the ISIC-2017 dataset and 98.97% on the PH2 dataset. These results indicate that the proposed method outperforms conventional detection models in terms of accuracy and other evaluation metrics.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for detection and classification in both ISIC-2017 and PH2 datasets. In ISIC-2017, 491 images (117 test, 30 validation, 374 training) were used. In PH2, 40 images were used.
Classification target in the ISIC-2017 dataset. 344 images (90 test, 42 validation, 254 training) were used. Also mentioned in introduction as a benign skin cancer.

Classification target in the ISIC-2017 dataset. 1,784 images (393 test, 78 validation, 1,372 training) were used. The paper groups various benign conditions under this label for the ISIC-2017 experiment.
Classification target in the PH2 dataset. 80 images were used.
Classification target in the PH2 dataset. 80 images were used.
Listed in the introduction as examples of benign skin cancers that do not spread, but not used as specific experimental classes in the proposed model's classification task.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion masks from dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The fuzzy based SegNet model performs precise segmentation of skin lesions from dermoscopic images. Performance is evaluated using Jaccard coefficient and Dice coefficient on ISIC-2017 and PH2 datasets.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The normalized stacked LSTM network classifies skin lesions into discrete classes. For the ISIC-2017 dataset, the classes are melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and benign. For the PH2 dataset, the classes are melanoma, atypical nevi, and common nevi. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, precision, and MCC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2017
datasets_used.description: An online benchmark dataset comprising dermoscopic images used for melanoma skin cancer detection. It contains images belonging to three classes: melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and benign.

use.training: Used for training the fuzzy based SegNet model and the normalized stacked LSTM network.
use.validation: Used for validation of the fuzzy based SegNet model and the normalized stacked LSTM network.
use.testing: Used for testing the fuzzy based SegNet model and the normalized stacked LSTM network.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset encompassing dermoscopic images with a pixel resolution of 768 × 560. It includes medical annotations such as histological and clinical diagnosis, assessment of dermoscopic criteria, and medical segmentation of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the fuzzy based SegNet model and the normalized stacked LSTM network.
use.validation: Used for validation of the fuzzy based SegNet model and the normalized stacked LSTM network.
use.testing: Used for testing the fuzzy based SegNet model and the normalized stacked LSTM network.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed model and conventional models were based on empirical evaluation metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, precision, MCC, Jaccard coefficient, Dice coefficient) without statistical significance testing.

segmentation.notes: The fuzzy based SegNet model was compared with five conventional segmentation models. It achieved the highest scores.

classification.notes: The normalized stacked LSTM network was compared with five traditional classification models. It achieved the highest accuracy of 98.98%. The classes were melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and benign.

segmentation.notes: The fuzzy based SegNet model was compared with five conventional segmentation models. It achieved the highest scores.

classification.notes: The normalized stacked LSTM network was compared with five traditional classification models. It achieved the highest accuracy of 98.97%. The classes were melanoma, atypical nevi, and common nevi.

architecture_used.main_model: Fuzzy based SegNet model for segmentation; Normalized stacked LSTM network for classification; ResNet-50 for feature extraction
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-50 model used for feature extraction (implies pretraining, but specific pretraining dataset not explicitly stated in context)

segnet_structure.encoder: Thirteen convolutional layers, inspired by VGG-16
segnet_structure.decoder: Maps lower-resolution encoder feature maps for pixel-wise classification using pooling indices for non-linear upsampling
segnet_structure.loss_function: Cross entropy loss combining output of clustering algorithm and SegNet model

feature_extractor.name: ResNet-50
feature_extractor.layers: 49 convolutional layers and one fully connected layer

classification_model.name: Normalized stacked LSTM network
classification_model.structure: Multiple hidden LSTM layers with a normalization layer added to every LSTM layer
classification_model.purpose: To reduce internal covariate shift, overfitting risk, and exploding/vanishing gradient problems

classification_or_output_head.classification_output: Skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Seborrheic keratosis, Benign for ISIC-2017; Melanoma, Atypical nevi, Common nevi for PH2)

conclusions: The proposed automated system, combining a fuzzy-based SegNet model for segmentation and a normalized stacked LSTM network for classification, effectively detects melanoma skin cancer.
The fuzzy-based SegNet model, which integrates deep fuzzy clustering with SegNet, provides robust segmentation performance, handling noise, artifacts, and fuzzy boundaries in dermoscopic images.

The fuzzy-based SegNet model achieved a Jaccard coefficient of 0.98 and Dice coefficient of 0.99 on the ISIC-2017 dataset, and a Jaccard coefficient of 0.97 and Dice coefficient of 0.98 on the PH2 dataset, outperforming conventional segmentation models.
The normalized stacked LSTM network improves feature abstraction by learning hierarchical representations over temporal and spatial dimensions, enhancing generalization and classification precision.

The normalized stacked LSTM network achieved an accuracy of 98.98% on the ISIC-2017 dataset and 98.97% on the PH2 dataset, surpassing traditional classification models such as ANN, RNN, GRU, Bi-LSTM, and stacked LSTM.
The five-fold cross-validation technique was identified as the most effective for reducing overfitting and bias in the proposed model.
The study acknowledges that the normalized stacked LSTM network has limited spatial understanding, which is proposed to be addressed in future work by incorporating a spatial attention process.
important_remarks: The study relies on two public benchmark datasets (ISIC-2017 and PH2) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.
The PH2 dataset is very small (200 images total), which may lead to overfitting or inflated performance metrics compared to larger datasets.
The ISIC-2017 dataset is imbalanced, with 1,372 benign images versus only 374 melanoma images in the training set, potentially biasing the model towards the majority class despite the use of MCC.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance differences between the proposed model and baselines.
The paper acknowledges a limitation of the normalized stacked LSTM network regarding 'limited spatial understanding,' suggesting this is a known architectural weakness.

Data leakage risks are present as the same datasets (ISIC-2017 and PH2) are used for both segmentation and classification tasks without explicit mention of strict patient-level separation between training and testing splits for the two stages.
The implementation was conducted using MATLAB R2020b, which may limit reproducibility for researchers using standard deep learning frameworks like PyTorch or TensorFlow.
No data augmentation techniques were reported, which is notable given the small size of the PH2 dataset and the potential for overfitting.
